action.thought_summary: Starting with the core interaction model since 'multiple users simultaneously' is ambiguous - could mean a single shared screen or multiple stations. This foundational question will shape all subsequent requirements.
action.question: When you say multiple users can interact simultaneously, do you envision a single large shared touchscreen where several people interact at once, or multiple separate display stations that each serve one user at a time?

action.thought_summary: User clarified multi-user setup as separate stations. Now probing interaction behavior, specifically how users interact with 3D models since that's a complex interaction type with many implicit requirements around gestures, controls, and feedback.
action.question: How should users interact with the 3D models — for example, should they be able to rotate, zoom, and pan freely, or are there predefined views or guided tours they can step through? And what input method will they use (touchscreen gestures, physical controllers, gesture recognition)?

action.thought_summary: User wants independent stations with free zoom/rotate on 3D models, undecided on input method. Since this is a museum, visitor demographics and accessibility are critical for interaction design. Probing this will inform input method recommendations and interaction constraints.
action.question: Since this is a museum setting, what range of visitors should the system accommodate — for example, will children, elderly visitors, or people with motor impairments be using it? This will help determine appropriate input methods and interaction complexity.

action.thought_summary: User wants focus on interaction design or visual style. I've covered multi-user setup and 3D interaction basics. Now probing how users navigate and discover content - this bridges interaction and content categories per the coverage schedule.
action.question: How should users discover and navigate between the different multimedia items — for example, would they browse through categories or collections, follow a curated storyline, or search by keywords? And should the system remember where a user left off if they step away and return later?

action.thought_summary: User has twice requested questions about visual style/interface design. I should honor this and probe the visual aesthetic direction for the museum display system.
action.question: Should the visual design of the interface match or complement the museum's existing exhibit aesthetic and branding, or should it have a distinctly modern digital look that stands apart from the physical exhibits? For example, would a minimalist dark-background design with high-contrast imagery suit the environment, or something more ornate that blends with traditional museum displays?

action.thought_summary: Style preferences have been captured (sky blue background, navy UI elements). Interaction basics covered (separate stations, zoom/rotate). Content/data area is underexplored - need to probe about how multimedia content is managed, updated, and structured in the system.
action.question: How will the multimedia content — images, text, and 3D models — be loaded and updated in the system? For example, will museum staff upload content through an administrative interface, will it pull from an existing collection database, or will it be pre-loaded and rarely changed?

action.thought_summary: User has specified multi-station setup, free zoom/rotate interaction, sky blue + navy color scheme. They redirect to interaction/visual style when unsure about operational details. I should probe interaction design further - specifically how different media types are presented together and what happens during interaction, as this could reveal implicit requirements about layout, transitions, or feedback.
action.question: When a user selects a multimedia item to examine, how should the different content types be presented together? For example, if an exhibit item has both a 3D model and descriptive text, should the text appear alongside the model in a split view, or should users toggle between viewing the model and reading the text? Also, should there be any visual feedback or animations when users interact with items, such as highlighting or smooth transitions?

action.thought_summary: The user has repeatedly deflected broad questions and asked for more specific interaction design or visual style questions. I need to ask a very concrete, focused question about one specific aspect of interaction design or visual style. I'll probe the main browsing interface layout - specifically how items are presented for selection on the home screen.
action.question: When a user first approaches a display station, how should the available multimedia items be presented for selection — for example, as a grid of thumbnail cards, a horizontally scrolling carousel, or a categorized list with expandable sections?

action.thought_summary: The user keeps deflecting broad questions but reveals specific preferences when asked concretely (e.g., sky blue/navy colors). I should ask a very specific, concrete question about interaction design or visual style to uncover more implicit requirements. The 3D model viewer is a key interaction element worth probing specifically.
action.question: When a user is examining a 3D model, should the rotation and zoom controls be visible as on-screen buttons (like +/− icons and a rotation indicator), or should the interface rely entirely on direct touch gestures like pinch-to-zoom and drag-to-rotate with no visible control buttons?

action.thought_summary: The user has been deflecting many specific design questions. I should try a different angle - probing for implicit requirements about museum-specific behaviors like idle state, attract mode, or session timeouts that they may not have considered but are common in museum installations.
action.question: When a display station is not being used by anyone, should it show an attract screen or idle animation to draw visitors in, and should there be an automatic timeout that resets the station to this idle state after a period of inactivity?

action.thought_summary: The user has consistently deflected specific questions about layout, navigation, controls, and idle behavior, saying they're not sure. action.question: You mentioned sky blue backgrounds and navy interface elements. For the text content displayed in the system, should the body text be dark (like black or dark navy) for readability against the sky blue, or would you prefer white text with a semi-transparent navy panel behind it for contrast?

action.thought_summary: User has given few concrete answers beyond: separate stations, free zoom/rotate, sky blue background with navy elements. They keep deflecting to ask for more specific interaction/style questions but then deflect those too. I should try a very specific, concrete question about an aspect not yet covered. Audio/sound is a key multimedia element not discussed and highly relevant to museums.
action.question: Should the display stations include audio capabilities — for example, playing narrated descriptions or sound effects when a user selects an item? If so, should audio play through built-in speakers or require visitors to use headphones to avoid disturbing others in the museum?

action.thought_summary: The user has been consistently deferring specific design decisions. I've covered interaction (stations, zoom/rotate), style (sky blue/navy), and partially content. action.thought_summary: The user has consistently deflected nearly all specific design and interaction questions, saying they're unsure and need to explore further. After 19 turns, the only concrete requirements elicited are: separate display stations, free zoom/rotate for 3D models, sky blue background, and navy interface elements. The turn budget is nearly exhausted with diminishing returns, so I should finish.
action.finish_summary: Elicited requirements for the museum multimedia display system: (1) Multiple separate display stations or distinct interaction areas so users can engage independently without interfering with each other. (2) Users can freely zoom and rotate multimedia content including 3D models to examine items in detail. (3) Visual style: sky blue background with navy-colored interface elements (buttons, menus, navigation components) for a clean, modern look comfortable for extended browsing. (4) Input method is undetermined but should be appropriate for a museum setting. Many design details—including navigation structure, content layout, control methods, accessibility, audio, screen orientation, typography, animations, idle behavior, and content management—remain unspecified and would need further exploration.
